comments:
Summary
This paper studies a general "compare-aggregate" architecture for matching two text sequences and systematically evaluates six different word-level comparison functions (NN, NTN, EUCCOS, SUB, MULT, SUBMULT+NN). The model uses a preprocessing gating layer, a one-directional attention to build for each word in one sequence an attention-weighted representation of the other sequence, a comparison function applied per word pair, and a CNN to aggregate the per-word comparison vectors for final classification/scoring. The model is evaluated on four datasets/tasks (MovieQA, InsuranceQA, WikiQA, SNLI) and the authors report competitive or state-of-the-art results in several cases; they find element-wise comparison functions (SUB, MULT, or their combination) perform best overall. Code is released.

Strengths
- The paper clearly frames the problem and situates the model within the popular compare-aggregate family of models. The architectural description is straightforward and easy to follow.
- Systematic empirical exploration of alternative word-level comparison functions is useful: considering element-wise SUB and MULT alongside more flexible NN/NTN and similarity measures (euclidean/cosine) is informative. The finding that simple element-wise operations often match or outperform more complex parametrized comparators is a useful empirical takeaway.
- Evaluation on four diverse datasets (two answer-selection/ranking tasks, one machine comprehension, one textual entailment) supports claims of generality. Ablations indicating the roles of preprocessing and attention are helpful. Code availability is a plus for reproducibility.

Weaknesses / Concerns
- Novelty is limited. The model closely follows prior compare-aggregate and attention-based designs (e.g., decomposable attention, match-LSTM, pairwise interaction models). The primary novelty is the systematic comparison of word-level comparison functions and the finding that element-wise SUB/MULT perform well. This is an incremental contribution, not a fundamentally new architecture.
- Experimental comparisons rely on reported numbers for prior work rather than reimplementations under identical conditions. Without controlled retraining / matched hyperparameter settings it is difficult to judge whether gains come from the architecture, the comparison function choice, implementation/hyperparameter choices, or data preprocessing. The authors note they did not re-implement baselines, which weakens empirical claims.
- Some methodological details are missing or under-specified: how window sizes and number of filters in the CNN aggregator were selected; precise dimensionalities and whether/how padding/truncation was handled; whether class imbalances were addressed; exact training/early-stopping criteria; and runtime / memory costs. These matter for reproducing and assessing scalability (e.g., MovieQA with long plots).
- Word embeddings are frozen (GloVe) in all experiments; the choice is reasonable but freezing may interact with the comparison function. It is unclear whether updating embeddings would change relative performance of comparison functions. No experiments test this.
- Statistical significance of reported gains is not provided. Many improvements appear modest; it is unclear which are robust.
- The preprocessing layer is order-insensitive (element-wise gating). The paper notes that for some tasks an order-sensitive preprocessing (RNN) was only used for SNLI but does not systematically evaluate the impact of using context-aware encoders (BiLSTM/CNN) for all tasks. This leaves open whether the strong performance owes to word-by-word comparison only because context encoding was limited.

Suggestions for authors
- Re-run key baselines under the same codebase and training protocol (or clearly report any differences) so comparisons are fair. At minimum, re-implement the strongest competing models or include an oracle baseline to quantify gains from architecture vs training details.
- Report statistical significance (e.g., bootstrap / paired tests) for improvements, and include variance (std dev) over multiple runs to show stability.
- Provide more ablation experiments: (a) allow embeddings to be fine-tuned and examine whether the benefit of SUB/MULT persists; (b) compare one-directional vs two-directional attention; (c) evaluate the effect of using context-sensitive preprocessing (BiLSTM) for all tasks rather than only SNLI; (d) study sensitivity to CNN aggregator hyperparameters.
- Include runtime/memory costs and parameter counts for each comparison function to make trade-offs clearer.
- Expand error analysis: show representative failure cases and examine when SUB/MULT help vs when parametrized NN/NTN are beneficial.

Verdict
This paper offers useful empirical findings within an established line of work. The main scientific contribution is systematic evaluation of word-level comparison functions and the empirical takeaway that simple element-wise operations perform strongly across varied sequence-matching tasks. However, the novelty is incremental and the empirical evaluation could be strengthened (controlled baselines, significance tests, more ablations). I recommend acceptance (score 7) provided the authors address experimental rigor and provide additional analyses/ablations in the final version.
